title: The US Jury System
topic: 陪审团制度与民主改革
passage:
Many Americans regard the jury system as a concrete expression of crucial democratic values. These include the principles that citizens meeting minimal qualifications of age and literacy are equally competent to serve; that jurors should be selected randomly from a representative cross section of the community; that no citizen should be denied the right to serve on account of race, religion, sex or national origin; that defendants are entitled to trial by their peers; and that verdicts should represent the conscience of the community rather than only the letter of the law. The jury is also said to be the best surviving example of direct rather than representative democracy.

But as recently as 1968, jury selection procedures conflicted with these democratic ideals. In some states jury duty was limited to persons of supposedly superior intelligence, education and moral character. Although the Supreme Court had prohibited intentional racial discrimination in jury selection as early as the 1880 case Strauder v. West Virginia, selecting so-called elite or blue-ribbon juries provided a convenient way around antidiscrimination laws.

The system also failed to regularly include women on juries until the mid-20th century. Although women first served on state juries in Utah in 1898, it was not until the 1940s that a majority of states made women eligible. Several states automatically exempted women unless they personally asked to be included. This was justified by the claim that women were needed at home, and it kept juries unrepresentative of women through the 1960s.

In 1968 Congress passed the Jury Selection and Service Act, ushering in democratic reforms. It abolished special educational requirements for federal jurors and required random selection from a cross section of the community. In Taylor v. Louisiana (1975), the Supreme Court extended the requirement of representative juries to the state level and declared sex discrimination in jury selection unconstitutional.

questions.questionText: From the principles of the US jury system, we learn that ______.

options.A: both literate and illiterate people can serve on juries
options.B: defendants are immune from trial by their peers
options.C: no age limit should be imposed for jury service
options.D: judgment should consider the opinion of the public

questions.explanation: 原文规定裁决应代表社区良知，而非仅是法律条文，因此 D 符合。
questions.commonMistake: 忽略 minimal qualifications 表明年龄和识字能力仍有门槛。

questions.questionText: The practice of selecting so-called elite jurors prior to 1968 showed _____.

options.B: the prevalent discrimination against certain races
options.C: the conflicting ideals in jury selection procedures
options.D: the arrogance common among the Supreme Court judges

questions.explanation: 精英陪审团制度绕开了既有反歧视法规，说明法律本身在执行上存在漏洞。
questions.commonMistake: 把‘绕开法律’直接等同于只针对某一种族的歧视。

questions.questionText: Even in the 1960s, women were seldom on jury lists in some states because _____.

options.A: they were automatically banned by state laws
options.B: they fell far short of the required qualifications
options.C: they were supposed to perform domestic duties
options.D: they tended to evade public engagement

questions.explanation: 原文明确写到这种做法的理由是女性被认为需要留在家中。
questions.commonMistake: 将 automatically exempted 误解为法律永久禁止。

questions.questionText: After the Jury Selection and Service Act was passed, _____.

options.A: sex discrimination in jury selection was unconstitutional and had to be abolished
options.B: educational requirements became less rigid in the selection of federal jurors
options.C: jurors at the state level ought to be representative of the entire community
options.D: states ought to conform to the federal court in reforming the jury system

questions.explanation: 1968 年法案废除了联邦陪审员的特殊教育要求；性别歧视和州级代表性要求是 1975 年判决的内容。
questions.commonMistake: 混淆 1968 年法案与 1975 年 Taylor v. Louisiana 判决。

questions.questionText: In discussing the US jury system, the text centers on _______.
questions.explanation: 文章先介绍制度体现的民主传统，再按时间说明其历史问题和改革发展。
questions.commonMistake: 只抓住中段的问题，忽略首尾的传统与改革脉络。
